speaker: interviewer
text: You know what, I will touch you here, it means we have still two minutes to go. So you can-- after a minute you can stop, because it's already over 30. November 30, '94 Survivor is Edith Singer Slomovits. Interviewer Dana Schwartz. It's in Los Angeles, and the language will be English.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is Dana Schwartz. It's November 30, 1994. And I'm speaking to Edith Singer. Edith, start by saying your name and where you're from.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Edith Singer. And my maiden name is Slomovits. I'm from Chust, [NON-ENGLISH]. When I was born, it was Czechoslovakia. And the outbreak of war, World War II, in '39, it became Hungary. And now it's Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's right. What would-- did your mother call you and what did your friends call you when you were little?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I had a-- a whole bunch of names. And officially in school, I was called Ethel [? Bertha ?] Slomovits on the Czechs. And because I was very little-- I was always the smallest all the friends-- so the-- my friends' mothers used to call me Bertiska. It was cute till a certain point, but once-- once I realized I'm not Bertiska, and I wanted to be a big girl. And at home, my real Yiddish name was Etya So I asked once my father, how did I get to Ethel [? Bertha. ?] So he-- in-- in those days, a Jew went to register a newborn baby, they gave the names. And sometimes they made fun.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they added, because Ethel-- Ethel [? Bertha ?] was my name-- Ethel to Etya is close, but [? Bertha ?] has no connection and no relationship. But probably the-- the clerk there, he decided to add another name. So this is how I-- I got those names.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after the war, I didn't like my name, so I changed it to-- I went to Israel and people told me Edith is Judith in-- in Hebrew, so I became Judith. And I always felt Judith is not me. And then when we came later to the United States and I went I made my citizen papers, then I decided one name, Edith Singer. And no middle name and no [? Bertha ?] and anything. Edith goes in Hungarian and Czech and Hebrew. Hebrew, Edith is a very nice name. It means a very-- the most fertile soil. It's called Edith. So I like my name now very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Edith, go back as far as you can and tell me about your family history. About your grandparents, or great grandparents. Who were they? Where did they live? What did they do? Any pieces of information at all, even if they're small.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. My mother was born in Chust. And my father was born in-- in [INAUDIBLE]. This is also [NON-ENGLISH] on the border of Shiget, Romania. So my father had-- was a border town, Romania and Czechoslovakia. And later was Romania and Hungary. My father's family was very famous because my grandmother had 10 sons and no daughters. He was the third son. My grandmother was a very little woman and very busy, because she had a grocery store. She had fields. She had chickens and geese and-- and turkeys and whatever you can imagine the backyard. Even though she had two maids, a Jewish maid who helped in the kitchen to cook, and a non-Jewish girl who helped her and others. But she was busy all the time. I don't know, a little woman, how she could work so much. My grandfather was a tall man with a long beard, a Hasid. And he was like a maharaja. He didn't help too much. He went to visit his sons from time to time. And the children usually-- most of the grandchildren liked grandma more than grandfather. And even today, when we talk about him-- some of us who have survived. And this grandfather had a mother. Nobody knows how old she was. And I used to go every summer to [NON-ENGLISH] to grandmother, to my grandparents, and then I was friends with my cousins. And [DRINKS] every time I was there, even when I was eight, nine years old, and later on, I always went to visit my great grandmother. And I remember her an old lady. She was always in bed. Sometimes she walked around. And when I came I had to tell her exactly who I am, who my father is, is because she had hundreds of great great grandchildren. So we have to always explain to her who my father is and who my mother is. And then she'll put her hand in-- in the pocket and give me [NON-ENGLISH], cube-- the sugar cubes. And even though we had enough of it at home, but when she gave it to me, it was very special. So special that I remember it till today. And then, every Friday, my grandmother used to bake challah and fish. And even though she had enough-- she lived with her youngest daughter-- she always felt that she wants to send to her mother from her challah, from her fish. So when I was there in the summer, we used-- I used to go bring it to her. And one of the visits, she showed me the tachrichim she had prepared. Tachrichim is the clothes that a person, when he dies, he puts it on. They put it on, he doesn't do it anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: They put it on him. And also, they have a little bit of eretz Yisrael, soil, soil from Israel. So she had it ready. She was probably in her 90s. She had the clothes what a-- that person wears and the soil. But she ended up in Auschwitz. She lived long enough that she didn't die and didn't use these clothes and the-- the soil from the Holy Land. So this was my great grandmother. My other grandparents, my mother's family, was more complicated. My grandmother was a very beautiful woman. She had four children with her husband and then he died. I don't know anymore from what he died. Probably was sick. And then she remarried a yeshiva bocher. And she had seven more children. And even though it was a very rich family, there was always a difference in the children. Especially by my grandfather. Her children, our children. Yeah. And she felt that she grew up with a real-- this is how heard always the stories with grandfather-- I always say like in a-- the stories-- in the old-fashioned stories when you describe a-- a-- a step-father or step-mother. She was only two years old when she lost her father. She hardly remembered him. And when she married-- when I go back, I was in my grandparents' house, I remember once when my grandmother was still alive, and we figured out, I was probably three or four years old, and I went to her at Shabbat morning. And this is the picture I remember. Nothing else about this grandmother. She was in a wheelchair. She suffered from a very serious arthritis. So she was in a wheelchair. And she wheeled herself to the credenz and opened it and took out some cake and gave me. This was customary. When grandchild came, she gave me cake. That's what I remember from my maternal grandmother. And then when she died, my step-grandfather remarried and had another child with this woman. And something happened in the family, because we never went there. We were not in speaking terms. And I think what happened, that my father-- after he married my mother and years later-- he realized it was such a rich home-- even though they were 11 children. That when my grandmother died, she wanted her inheritance. And it was a-- a-- quite an amount, because otherwise I don't think they would have gone-- they went to court with it. And it was like half of the property, what belonged to my grandmother, to divide in 11 parts. And still, it was a substantial amount, because my father went to court and he won it. And my step-grandfather had to pay every time-- every month some money. I don't know the details. But I think this brought it out that my mother-- her sisters and brothers didn't speak to her. The-- the half-sisters and half-brothers. And even after the war, till today, three of her sisters still are alive. And they very seldom talk about my mother. And they feel like an anger towards her and my grandfather. And I think all goes back to this part of her life.

speaker: interviewer
text: [DRINKS] So one of your grandmothers was-- raised fowl-- geese and ducks and so on. And that's how she made money. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they had also a store.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: A grocery store.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's mother married a wealthy guy.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. The grandmother was the wealthy one.

speaker: interviewer
text: The grandmother-- the--

speaker: interviewee
text: The guy wasn't very-- he was a yeshiva bocher, a good-looking one. And she had a-- a store-- I don't know how you call it in English-- a [NON-ENGLISH], they called it at home. Wine, and liquor, and wholesale.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because next-- I remember the store. And next door was a store like a bar-- not as nice as here is a bar-- where one of the sons it was his-- my mother's oldest brother. This was his-- his store that he selled-- he sold wine for people. Like, for every Shabbat we went there for the wine. And also when the peasants came in, he will sell them a drink. And then later, under the Hungarians, we had a lot of stories what happened to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I grew up with several languages. At home, we spoke Yiddish, mainly with my father. With my mother, Hungarian. Because when my-- my parents were born at the turn of the century, this part of Czechoslovakia belonged to Austria-Hungary. My mother also spoke fluently German, and I was exposed to German, and I learned it in school. Also, Czech in school. My parents didn't speak Czech too well, but we did, the children. And the peasants were Russians. And where my father grew up, the peasants were Romanians. So I was really exposed to all those languages. And later in life it was very handy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in concentration camp I could communicate with people in German and in Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when the war broke out in-- in--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 12 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. So, tell me about the schools you attended until then.

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to Czech public school. And in the afternoons, I went to [? Bais ?] Yaakov school, a-- a Jewish school, to learn to pray and to read and write in Yiddish. And even though everybody knows how-- how great the Czechs are, they were always considered the second biggest democracy after the United States, I go back till my kindergarten that I was exposed to-- to antisemitism in kindergarten. I remember that we went together with Jewish and non-Jewish children. But in kindergarten-- and I was probably five-- we had separate tables. In the front were sitting the Czech children, whose parents were the-- the clerks and-- and the leaders of the-- of the city. Then they had tables for other non-Jewish children. Not Czech, but the Hungarian, Germans, whatever. And in the end, the Jewish children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remember sitting separate table only at the end, because I was Jewish in Czechoslovakia. Then, in elementary school, I remember that the teacher always came and looked with a pencil under our ears to look for lice. Only at the Jewish children. Not the others. So we were exposed already as-- as-- I always felt, as far as I can go in memory, that I was Jewish. I wasn't-- Are you saying that-- that the-- that Jewish children usually had more lice? Or are you saying that they-- why would they not have checked the peasant children? But they would check the Jewish children for lice?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were Jewish children who came from a poor neighborhood. Maybe they had some lice. I don't know if the non-Jewish had lice, because I only remember that they looked at the Jewish children. And I don't think they had more lice than the non-Jewish children, but this is one way to-- to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Put them down.

speaker: interviewee
text: Put-- put us down, yeah. Then in elementary school I remember also-- we had, in Czechoslovakia-- I'm still talking about Czechoslovakia before '39-- boy Jewish children had separate classes in elementary schools. A Jewish class and a non-Jewish class. And I remember in fourth grade the principal came in and picked out four girls who were straight-A students and took them to the non-Jewish class so they will lift up the level of the class.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he took out, but-- but the rest we stayed in the class. So those are my memories from-- from the Czech democracy. But what happened later, it was quite good. What do you mean? What happened later when the war broke up and the Hungarians came in, and then the Germans. So-- but-- I mean, under the Czechs, we-- my father had his business. He was in the lumber business. And we could-- we-- we were free people, even though in certain places and certain parts, we were discriminated or we didn't feel comfortable. But officially, we were citizens like everybody else. Would you-- if you walked down the street, would you-- would the children be unpleasant to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never had really, a close relationship with non-Jewish children in Chust ever.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what about strange children, if they knew you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: With girls wasn't so bad as with boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it with boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: With boys-- the-- the non-Jewish boys, the [NON-ENGLISH], used to run after the Jewish boys and hit them. And they used to go and fight. This was kind-- like-- like an accepted thing. But not with girls. It was mainly with boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, what was your family like? Were they very religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My father was a Hasid. Hasid. My grandfather, all his sons. And they were Hasidim from the Shiget [NON-ENGLISH]. And strict orthodox. My mother wore a sheitel. My father wore a shtreimel that my mother hated.

speaker: interviewer
text: A hat.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, a shtreimel. I don't know how you--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a hat with a fur edges.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fur edges, yes, that's the shtreimel. And she didn't like it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he wear a black [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, for Shabbat. When he wore the shtreimel, he had a [NON-ENGLISH], but otherwise he wore a-- a suit. But everything was black. His hat was black. The suit, the shoes. He had about 20 pairs of shoes. And when he was-- he was actually out of town, his business-- and he used to come home for a lumber mill. I have two lumber mills, it's a brother, together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So used to go to the villages near the [NON-ENGLISH], the highest point of the [NON-ENGLISH], there, where the forests were. And then for the weekends and holidays, he used to come home. So when he came home, he told me to bring him the black shoes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he will tell me which kind. And I never knew, because they looked, to me, all the same. But he recognized them. It was so strange to me, till today, if I think back, how he knew which pair of shoes he wanted. And they were all the same. High, black shoes. But he was very neat and clean, had very neat beard and always tip-top. Even though, if I think back, to me, it looked like he always wears the same thing. Because he didn't wear a hat that was brown or gray, just black.

speaker: interviewer
text: [DRINKS] What-- how-- how did you first know that something was wrong, politically?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I always heard my parents, and the adults in general, talking about World War I. They were discussing it. And right away, every conversation ended with will there be another war?

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was exposed to those-- those subjects without understanding it. Also, I heard the name Hitler. Probably started '33, when I was only six years old. I heard the name, and I really didn't understand it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I heard it so much that I had once a dream. And I remember till today. I dreamt that Hitler came to our house and he made a fire in the oven. That's all what I remember from the dream. And later when I went through all this, I said, what a-- a-- a sign it was-- what-- what a coincidence that I had this dream. Before even the war started. I just heard so much about Hitler. But otherwise, I didn't know too much.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were they talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were talking about what he used to-- to-- his speeches about the Jewish people. How he plans to destroy them, to kill them. And everybody was scared. But somehow, nobody really thought it will happen. And when it really happened, it was too late.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to go back when the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, 1939 when World War II broke out, I remember that Poland was attacked first. And after three weeks they-- they lost the war. And I remember two days and two nights how the people from the Polish army-- soldiers and officers on bicycles and on horseback and in wagons-- they went through our city. We were standing out there looking. And I was watching. It was interesting, because as a little town and so much commotion. And I really didn't understand the seriousness of the situation. I just watched, because so many people were marching on-- on the streets. And then, later, we started to hear what was going on in Poland. What really happened-- this was '39. In '41, yeah, then we were occupied by the Hungarians. After the-- the-- the Polish left and one day we were occupied by the Hungarians and then our lives changed very, very dramatically and drastically at once. Because under the Hungarians, we were still at home. But, we had million restrictions. First, I will tell you is what affected me. I was first year junior high school when the Hungarians came in. And then I was the last one that I was allowed to finish the three years of junior high school. All the children were younger than I, one year or more, couldn't attend any more public school. So the parents had to make a private schools and so they could go on with their studies. Then, when I finished junior high school, I wanted very badly to go to gymnasium. And were a very famous, huge gymnasium with all the kids from the neighbor villages used to come there. My mother took me-- I had a beautiful report card, one of the best-- and took me to the principal. And looked at it and he said, oh what a beautiful report card. I would love to have in school. But I cannot take you because you are Jewish. So I couldn't go. And my-- OK, I have to go back with  one thing, that under the Hungarians, a lot of rules came against the Jewish people. One of them-- first of all, they took away all the-- all the government positions from people. Then, they took away the radios, the bicycles. Every time something new came out, the men were-- the young men were drafted not in the army, but in the-- they call it [NON-ENGLISH], forced labor camps. They-- they wear their clothes, their own civilian clothes, but they had to wear a yellow armband. And they were taken to the frontiers to-- to work for the-- for the soldiers. They didn't fight the enemy, they were enemies too for the Hungarians. But they were doing the digging for the-- for the-- no-- for the soldiers, where they hide. I forgot--

speaker: interviewer
text: Fox holes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fox holes. And to serve them. And they-- they suffered hunger and-- and-- and-- and cold. And when they were taken, very seldom did we hear from them. So we never knew if those-- those people were-- were alive or not. [DRINKS] And then gradually, they took away all the businesses. And when they took away the businesses from everybody, suddenly we saw our neighbors, or people with whom we grew up, or my parents grew up, they just moved in, and different businesses. And then we realized how they looked at our Jewish business before. Because they moved in and businesses already with merchandise without paying a penny. They-- they just took it over. And we didn't get paid for it anything. So life became very difficult. But I remember that my father had another house, except the one that we lived in. And he sold it, and probably from this money, we lived. Some people made business. Black market was very risky, but they had to do something to keep alive and to keep their families alive. So life was very difficult under the Hungarians. But, I always say we are still together. We were together with family. And we were not hungry. We couldn't buy new clothes, or furniture, or anything. But we were always hoping the war would go to an end and then we'll go back to normal life.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like to mention some incidents under the Hungarians. My father had a beard for religious reasons. And one day he walked in the house without it. And I-- I don't know if I was the first one to see it. I was in shock, because I never saw my father without it. I said, what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said a Hungarian soldier grabbed him on the stairs and said, you, Jew, come with me. And he dragged him to a barber shop and told the barber, shave off the beard for this Jew. And he had to do it. So after this, my father didn't grow another beard, because he was afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the significance of the beard to a Jewish man?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a religious Jew. He was a Hasid. So after you get married, you don't shave. And this was different degrees. My-- some people, like my grandfather, had a beard he grew as it grew, free. But my father used to trim his beard. And he had friends who criticized him that he's not religious enough, because he's trimming his beard. So it was different degrees of-- in religion. No matter how religious you were, there was still somebody who was more or less.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, did your father hurt from this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, definitely. Not only him, the whole family. Because we felt so degraded. Because they knew that the beard is for religious reasons. This is why they did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, was emasculating him in some way.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Or just showing you cannot observe what you want, you cannot do what you want. We will tell you what to do. And another incident was with my uncle who had the bar. Peasants used come in to drink and they were very often drunk. And when they paid, he gave them change. And during the Hungarians, one man did it once, and they learned from it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he will give, let's say $5 and my uncle wants to give him back change from $5. He said, oh no, I gave you $20. He said, no, I-- you gave me $5. Then he will run out on the street, find a policeman, bring him back to the store, and say, I gave him $5-- $20 and he doesn't want to give me back. So the policeman forced my uncle to give him change from $20. And then put-- beat him up and put him to jail for a-- for a few days without asking any questions. And this was an everyday occurrence in different forms and ways. But, I'm just bringing this as an example how we suffered from antisemitism.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course in the school, I felt it also. Day in and day out. [DRINKS] From students and from the teachers. I remember one teacher very, very kindly, an older lady. And when we were hassled around or beaten up by other students because we are Jewish, we came to her. We told her. And she stopped the teaching and she would tell the students how wrong it is to do those things, how wrong it is to be prejudiced. And we are human beings. We shouldn't do those things. It helped for a half a day. Next day, everything went back. But still, I remember her very kindly. The other teacher was the other-- the other end of-- of the story. She was very antisemitic. And this was all in junior high school, under the Hungarians. One day-- she was the teacher of hand-- hand work, we called it, crocheting, embroidering, and also singing. And one day I came to school and I was very hoarse. I had real bad laryngitis. But I didn't stay home. And the minute she-- she heard me speaking, suddenly she looked at me. She called me to sing solo. And I hardly could talk, so I told her I cannot sing. She says yes, you will sing. And I said I cannot. And she started to scream at me. And I had started to sing. And I was singing, and my tears are rolling down my-- my face, because I was crying. And the whole place was laughing. And that was it. And I was very, very embarrassed. And never before and never after did she ever ask me to sing solo. Just this one day, when I was so hoarse. So I cannot forget it till today.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she made fun of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- yeah. So we-- we knew that she-- wherever she could harm a Jewish student, she did it, in different ways.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went on. We-- I came home. I told the story. We couldn't do anything. It wasn't like-- like you can go to the principal and complain. We're just-- we're glad we can live and-- with [? our ?]-- [? our ?]-- [? our ?] quiet. In 1941, a lot of people from our town, and also around [NON-ENGLISH] that used to be Czechoslovakia, all the Jewish families who didn't have a Czech, originally, or a Hungarian citizenship, they were expelled from the country. And they were taken to Poland. And we didn't know about them anything. But one family, who lived across the street from us, they came back, the whole family, parents and three children. The youngest daughter was my friend. She lives here in Los Angeles, and we talk about it sometimes. And they came back. And they told us the stories, how the Germans tell the Jewish people to dig their own graves. And they shoved them in. But it was so horrible that we didn't believe it. We didn't want to believe it. And I-- it was so-- I think it's human nature that we didn't want to believe it. And we-- mainly, we hoped it will not happen to us. And even though some people listened carefully and they tried to go away, it wasn't easy to leave the place and go away. Because the tragedy, the second part of the tragedy, was that nobody opened the gates for us to say, OK you are in danger in Hungary, or Germany, or Poland, come to us. So it wasn't easy. So we had to sit and wait and hope and pray.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you have gone to Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where could you have gone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe to Romania, or-- or-- or-- it was very hard, because in the minute the Hungarians came in, the borders were closed. So to go somewhere, you had to really go on-- hiding. And to go with your children-- it was-- it was too-- too difficult, or almost impossible. And to go to another country with papers officially, very few people were very lucky enough to get those papers and to-- to do it. I remember only one family who left from Chust to other places. But we were waiting. And we were home till 1944. In 1944, March, one day we woke up. The Germany occupied all of Hungary. And then the real trouble started.

speaker: interviewer
text: [DRINKS] Tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: One of the things that we survivors-- it's very, very typical when people used to ask us, and it happened to me a few  weeks again-- a few weeks ago again-- when they used to ask, why didn't you resist? That-- they asked me personally. They didn't ask my parents. They asked me. And I was a child at that time. And it was very hard to explain why we didn't resist. We did resist, first of all. And then, I always see today what's going on. And I believe the best example is the hostages in Iran. That the whole United States, with the mighty army behind them and they couldn't do too much. And then they ask a Jewish girl survivor from Hitler's death camp from Auschwitz why didn't I survive-- why didn't I resist.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.
